race.name: GFNY New York
race.tagline: The original Gran Fondo New York. 5,000 riders from 90+ countries start on the closed George Washington Bridge and race 100 miles through the Hudson Valley to Bear Mountain and back.

vitals.location: Fort Lee, New Jersey / Hudson Valley, New York, USA
vitals.registration: Online via gfny.cc. Tiered pricing increases monthly. Includes official race jersey, finisher medal, chip timing, aid stations, post-race meal.
vitals.entry_fee: ~$200-300 (tiered pricing, increases closer to race day)
vitals.feed_zones: Multiple fully stocked aid stations at miles ~17, ~32 (pre-Bear Mountain), ~55 (Bear Mountain summit), ~68 (Ramapo), and ~80 (West Nyack). Water, nutrition, mechanical support.
vitals.cutoff_time: Course support closes approximately 8.5-9 hours after start
route_options: GFNY Gran Fondo: ~100 miles, ~8,500 ft (full Bear Mountain route)
GFNY Medio: ~50 miles, ~3,500 ft (turns back before Bear Mountain)

climate.primary: Mid-May Northeast spring — mild with variable conditions
climate.description: Mid-May in the lower Hudson Valley delivers comfortable cycling weather most years. Temperatures typically range 55-75F at the start, warming to 65-80F by midday. The inland valleys can trap heat on warm years. Morning fog along the Hudson River is possible. Rain is the primary wild card — a cold, wet May day in the 50s with rain turns a gran fondo into a survival exercise. But most years, conditions are close to ideal for hard riding.
challenges: Rain risk: a cold, wet mid-May day drops temperatures into the 50s and makes descents treacherous
Morning chill on the GWB: standing on the bridge at 6:30 AM in cycling kit can be genuinely cold
Afternoon warming in inland valleys — mild dehydration risk on hot years
Wind exposure on the George Washington Bridge and along the Hudson River corridor

terrain.primary: Paved Hudson Valley roads with sustained climbs and rolling ridgeline terrain
terrain.surface: Paved roads throughout — Route 9W, county roads, and state park roads. Surface quality varies: 9W along the river is smooth; some of the Rockland County ridge roads have rougher patches. Bear Mountain's Perkins Memorial Drive is well-maintained but steep.
features: Bear Mountain: 4.5 miles, 1,260 ft gain, ~5% average — the course's signature climb, reaching 1,283 ft elevation
Alpine climb: 1 mile, 390 ft gain, 7.4% average with 10% peaks — early punch at mile 9
Gate Hill (Colle Andrea Pinarello): 2 miles at 4.9%, 12% maximum — the steepest sustained pitches on course
Cheesecote Mountain: 1 mile at 4.6% average, 18% maximum gradient — short but vicious
Leg-sapping Rockland County ridgeline: sharp climbs, longer drags, twisting descents between miles 45-68

fondo_rating.scoring_notes: Migrated from gravel-race-automation. Original score: 69. adventure dimension dropped, road_surface added. Score recalculated: 69.

course_description.character: An out-and-back loop that starts with one of cycling's most dramatic send-offs — 5,000 riders rolling across the closed George Washington Bridge — and then heads north through the Hudson Valley's mix of river flats, suburban rollers, and genuine mountain terrain. The first half builds toward Bear Mountain, the course's physical and emotional apex. The second half punishes you with the Rockland County ridgeline — a relentless sequence of sharp climbs and technical descents that hit when your legs are already compromised. The final miles back along 9W are a flat-road time trial to the finish in Fort Lee. The course rewards riders who can climb, recover on descents, and still produce power in the final 30 miles.
course_description.signature_challenge: Bear Mountain gets the name recognition, but the Rockland County ridgeline is the real race-breaker. Gate Hill and Cheesecote hit when you're 55-65 miles deep with 5,000+ feet already in the legs. The 18% maximum on Cheesecote stops riders who are already cooked. The course is designed to punish poor pacing — it's back-loaded by design.
suffering_zones: Dyckman Hill and the Run Home

logistics.airport: JFK International (JFK) — 1 hour to Fort Lee. Newark Liberty (EWR) — 30 minutes. LaGuardia (LGA) — 45 minutes. All three have direct flights from major cities worldwide. Newark is closest and easiest for Fort Lee access.
logistics.lodging_strategy: Fort Lee, NJ is the epicenter — walking distance to start/finish. Hotels along the GWB corridor (Fort Lee, Edgewater, Cliffside Park) book up early for race weekend. Manhattan is 20 minutes away via the GWB or A-train to 175th St. Budget travelers: look at hotels along I-95 in northern NJ. Book 2-3 months in advance for best rates.
logistics.food: It's New York City. The food situation needs no explanation. Fort Lee itself has excellent Korean restaurants and delis. Post-race meal is included with registration. Pre-race carb-loading options are limitless.

history.origin_story: Uli Fluhme first experienced the gran fondo concept in 1995 at the Oetztaler Radmarathon — 235 km with 5,000m of climbing in Austria. He and his partner Lidia recognized that nothing like it existed in the United States: American cycling events were either casual charity rides or closed-circuit criteriums, with nothing in between. They set out to bring authentic Italian-style gran fondo racing to New York City. Lidia negotiated directly with the Port Authority for access to the George Washington Bridge — the authority initially quoted $50,000, expecting to kill the idea, but the Fluhmes agreed. On May 8, 2011, 1,900 riders lined up on the GWB for the first GFNY. By 2012, the field had grown to 4,000+. The concept proved the market existed: competitive, timed, anti-doping-tested mass-participation cycling events in America. GFNY expanded internationally in 2014 (Italy and Mexico), and now operates 30+ events across 15 countries on 4 continents. The NYC event remains the World Championship — the crown jewel of the global calendar.
notable_moments: 2011: Inaugural GFNY on May 8 — 1,900 riders start on the George Washington Bridge for the first time
2012: Field doubles to 4,000+ riders; anti-doping testing introduced — a first for American gran fondos
2014: International expansion begins with GFNY Italia (Terracina) and GFNY Cozumel (Mexico)
2017-2019: Series grows to 20+ global events; NYC field stabilizes at ~5,000 from 90+ countries
2024: Gran Premio NYC launched — combining UCI professional racing with the amateur GFNY World Championship
2026: 16th edition — Michelob ULTRA GFNY World Championship NYC on May 17
history.reputation: The event that proved American gran fondos could be serious. The GWB start is iconic. The anti-doping program is genuine. The World Championship draws the most international amateur cycling field on the planet. GFNY NYC is to competitive gran fondos what the New York City Marathon is to mass-participation running — the flagship that defines the category.

biased_opinion.summary: GFNY New York is the race that proved American gran fondos could be taken seriously. When Uli and Lidia Fluhme paid the Port Authority $50,000 to close the George Washington Bridge for a cycling event in 2011, they were making a bet that the US market wanted European-style competitive cycling with real timing, real anti-doping, and real racing — not another charity ride with a fruit cup at the finish. Fifteen years and 30+ global editions later, that bet has paid off spectacularly. The NYC World Championship draws 5,000 riders from 90+ countries to race 100 miles through the Hudson Valley, summit Bear Mountain, and grind through the Rockland County ridgeline. The course is demanding without being mythical. The organization is professional without being corporate. And that start on the closed GWB — with the Manhattan skyline receding behind you as 5,000 riders roll out together — remains one of the most visually stunning moments in amateur cycling.
strengths: The George Washington Bridge start: the world's busiest bridge closed to traffic for 5,000 cyclists — nothing else in American cycling matches this visual
World Championship draws the deepest competitive field in the GFNY calendar, with anti-doping since 2012
Bear Mountain climb: 4.5 miles at 5% with summit views over the Hudson Highlands — a proper signature ascent
90+ countries represented — the most international amateur cycling event in the world
NYC logistics: three airports, unlimited lodging, world-class transit — getting here is the easy part
weaknesses: The course is suburban Northeast, not Alpine grandeur — much of the route passes through residential New Jersey and Rockland County
NYC-area expenses compound quickly: hotels, food, and parking at metropolitan pricing
Entry fee escalation: tiered pricing punishes late registrants, with costs approaching $300 near race day
Not technically challenging: paved roads throughout, no gravel sectors, no cobbled sections, no surface variety
biased_opinion.bottom_line: The GFNY World Championship in New York is the best competitive gran fondo in America — full stop. The GWB start is iconic, the course is honest, the organization is professional, and the international field gives it a cosmopolitan edge that no other US event matches. It's not L'Etape and it's not a Monument. But for a one-day test of fitness, strategy, and nerve on roads an hour from Times Square, GFNY NYC delivers exactly what it promises.

final_verdict.one_liner: 100 miles from the George Washington Bridge to Bear Mountain and back. The world championship of gran fondos, started by two people who paid $50K to close the busiest bridge in America.
final_verdict.should_you_race: Yes if you want the most competitive, most international gran fondo in North America. Yes if starting on a closed George Washington Bridge with the NYC skyline behind you is on your bucket list. Yes if you want anti-doping, real timing, and a world championship classification. Skip if you want Alpine grandeur — this is the Hudson Valley, not the Dolomites. Skip if NYC-area costs are a dealbreaker.
final_verdict.alternatives: If you want European-level climbing prestige: L'Etape du Tour. If you want a US gran fondo with bigger mountains: the Death Ride in California. If you want a GFNY event with less expense: GFNY Miami (flat) or any of the 30+ global GFNY locations.

videos.title: HOW TO CLIMB CHEESECOTE AT GFNY NYC
videos.description:
Cheesecote is the las climb at GFNY NYC.  Cheesecote's 18% grade is the steepest section of the GFNY NYC course. It comes right at the start of the climb. While short, it tends to suck enough energy to make the rest of the climb a drag. Good news is: once you crest the climb, the toughest part of GFNY is behind you. 
Luis Lemus and Flavio de Luna give us some tips and tactics on how to climb Cheesecote. 

#cycling #granfondo

videos.title: Exploring NYC pre and post race! // GFNY Word Championship NYC 2023
GFNY 2022 🗽 GRAN FONDO WORLD CHAMPIONSHIP NEW YORK CITY USA
videos.description: GFNY NYC serves as the GFNY World Championship and every May, cyclists descend onto New York City and the starting line on the world’s busiest bridge, George Washington Bridge, which is fully closed to cars for GFNY. The race features the most international peloton in the world with riders from almost 100 nations coming together from all corners of the world to challenge themselves against the clock and fellow riders over the iconic route. GFNY World Championship has become a mecca for road cyclists from around the world.
videos.curation_reason: High-view race recap showing the international scope and starting on the closed George Washington Bridge.

quotes.text: About 2500 people plus on the George Washington Bridge... I love love love this city. I would never debate anybody over what's the number one city in the world.

Cheesecote's 18% grade is the steepest section of the GFNY NYC course. It comes right at the start of the climb and tends to suck enough energy to make the rest of the climb a drag, but once you crest the climb, the toughest part of GFNY is behind you.

key_challenges.name: Gate Hill to Cheesecote combo
key_challenges.description: Gate Hill followed immediately by Cheesecote is described as crunch time and very tough. After Gate Hill, you turn left for a fast one-kilometer descent, then right turn into Cheesecote. This combo is where the peloton gets shattered and riders get dropped.

key_challenges.name: Cheesecote climb
key_challenges.description: 1.5 kilometers long with 5.2% average grade. The steepest ramps are double digits at the beginning. Described as the last very steep hill on the course and an attack point where riders make big differences.

key_challenges.name: Cobbles section
key_challenges.description: Riders encounter cobbles during the forest section, with one rider noting they went onto the cobbles and needed to get off quickly.

terrain_notes.text: Forest section provides a scenic break with sights, sounds, and smells. Cobbles are present and challenging to ride on.
After Gate Hill there's a fast, non-technical descent for one kilometer, followed by a small drag downhill before Cheesecote begins.

race_day_tips.text: Stay in good position and make sure to eat and hydrate before the climbs to make them as manageable as possible.
Don't waste everything on the Gate Hill to Cheesecote combo - save energy because there's still a long way to go to the finish with rolling hills remaining.
The Cheesecote climb is your last opportunity to make a big difference, but also the critical point where you don't want to get dropped.

additional_quotes.text: I want to give a shout out to Gustavo and his bike repair crew at the expo. They got us hooked up after our bikes needed tweaking from being on airplanes. They had them sounding bad when we unpacked them, but Gustavo's crew got these machines in premium condition. Very reasonable, personable, great representation for the event.

10 months of proper preparation and planning. It's game day, y'all. It's put up or shut up. It's going to take a few miles though for the nerves to come down with about 2500 people plus on the George Washington Bridge.

rider_intel.search_text: GFNY New York features over 2500 riders starting on the George Washington Bridge. The course includes a scenic forest section with challenging cobbles that riders struggle to navigate. The key difficulty comes from the Gate Hill to Cheesecote climb combination, described as crunch time where the peloton gets shattered. Gate Hill is followed immediately by a fast one-kilometer descent, then riders turn right into Cheesecote, a 1.5-kilometer climb averaging 5.2% grade with double-digit ramps at the start. This is considered the last very steep hill and a critical attack point where riders either make differences or get dropped. After this combo, rolling hills continue to the finish. The expo features bike mechanics who help riders whose bikes need adjustment after air travel.
